---
title: Build Your First App
description: Start from zero and reach a working runtime-managed app with one MCP tool.
---

The repo does not currently ship a `create-app` scaffold. The code-truth path is to create a workspace-local app, register it in `workspace.yaml`, and make the runtime health checks pass.

The first milestone is not "pretty UI". It is:

- the runtime can resolve the app
- the app starts through a supported lifecycle path
- the HTTP root passes
- the MCP health route passes
- one tool is registered and callable

## 1. Create the Workspace-Local App Directory

Start with this shape:

```text
<workspace-root>/
  workspace.yaml
  apps/
    my_app/
      app.runtime.yaml
      package.json
      src/
```

The runtime only cares that the workspace ends up with a valid `workspace.yaml` entry and an `app.runtime.yaml` at the declared path.

## 2. Write the Manifest First

Start with a minimal `apps/my_app/app.runtime.yaml`:

```yaml title="apps/my_app/app.runtime.yaml"
app_id: "my_app"
name: "My App"
slug: "my-app"

lifecycle:
  setup: "npm install"
  start: "npm run start"

healthchecks:
  mcp:
    path: /mcp/health
    timeout_s: 30
    interval_s: 5

mcp:
  transport: http-sse
  port: 13100
  path: /mcp/sse
  tools:
    - create_record

env_contract:
  - HOLABOSS_WORKSPACE_ID
```

Rules that matter immediately:

- `app_id` must match the workspace declaration exactly
- `mcp.port` is required by the current parser
- keep `mcp.path` explicit
- use either `lifecycle.start` or top-level `start`, not both lifecycle modes at once

## 3. Register the App in `workspace.yaml`

Add the runtime plan entry:

```yaml title="workspace.yaml"
applications:
  - app_id: my_app
    config_path: apps/my_app/app.runtime.yaml
```

`workspace-runtime-plan.ts` treats `applications[].app_id` and `applications[].config_path` as the app entrypoint. Duplicate app ids fail compilation.

## 4. Make the Health Contract Pass

Most first apps fail here.

The runtime probes:

- `GET http://localhost:$PORT/`
- `GET http://localhost:$MCP_PORT<healthchecks.mcp.path>`

So the app must expose:

- an HTTP root route that returns `2xx` or `3xx`
- an MCP health route that returns `200`

If either probe fails, the runtime will not mark the app healthy even if the process started cleanly.

## 5. Add One Real MCP Tool

Do not start with a wide tool surface. Add one tool that proves the end-to-end loop works.

Recommended first-tool shape:

1. accept small validated input
2. persist one local record
3. return machine-readable JSON
4. list the tool name under `mcp.tools`

If the manifest declares:

```yaml title="apps/my_app/app.runtime.yaml"
mcp:
  tools:
    - create_record
```

the runtime reconciles `workspace.yaml` so the visible allowlisted tool id becomes `my_app.create_record`.

## 6. Add Integrations Only When Needed

If the app needs a provider, declare `integrations` in `app.runtime.yaml` and use the [Bridge SDK](/docs/build/apps/bridge-sdk).

When bindings resolve, the runtime injects:

- `HOLABOSS_APP_GRANT`
- `HOLABOSS_INTEGRATION_BROKER_URL`
- `WORKSPACE_API_URL`
- `HOLABOSS_WORKSPACE_ID`

The app should call providers through `@holaboss/bridge`, not through direct provider tokens.

## 7. Pick the Right Launch Path

The runtime supports:

- shell lifecycle via `lifecycle.start`
- subprocess lifecycle via top-level `start`
- Docker Compose fallback when no native lifecycle is declared and a compose file exists

For a first app, native shell lifecycle is usually easiest to debug because the contract is explicit and the startup logs map directly to your manifest.

## 8. Start It Through the Runtime

Useful runtime surfaces while iterating:

- `POST /api/v1/apps/:appId/setup`
- `POST /api/v1/apps/:appId/start`
- `GET /api/v1/apps/:appId/build-status`
- `GET /api/v1/apps/:appId/setup-log`
- `GET /api/v1/apps/ports`

If you install from files or an archive instead of editing the workspace directly, the runtime also exposes:

- `POST /api/v1/apps/install`
- `POST /api/v1/apps/install-archive`

`install-archive` extracts into `apps/my_app/`, requires `app.runtime.yaml` at the archive root, appends the `workspace.yaml` application entry, and tries to reconcile the MCP registry after startup. It now serializes installs per `(workspace_id, app_id)` and returns `409` if another install for that app is already in progress.

When setup fails, pull the latest setup diagnostics:

```bash
curl \
  'http://127.0.0.1:5160/api/v1/apps/my_app/setup-log?workspace_id=workspace-1'
```

## 9. Verify the End-to-End Loop

Before adding more features, confirm:

- `workspace.yaml` points at the right manifest path
- `app.runtime.yaml` `app_id` matches the workspace entry
- the app serves `/`
- the MCP health route serves the configured health path
- the runtime can set up and start the app
- `workspace.yaml` contains entries such as `my_app.create_record` after startup when `mcp.tools` is declared

## Good First Milestone

Your first app is good enough when another developer can:

- clone the workspace
- start the app through the runtime
- hit the HTTP root successfully
- see the MCP health check pass
- call one tool
- inspect the created local record
- see the tool registered in `workspace.yaml`

Everything after that is feature depth, not proof that the app fits the runtime model.
